column1: What problem does the Strategy Pattern solve?
column2: It lets you swap algorithms/behaviors without changing the caller code.

column1: When should I use Strategy Pattern?
column2: Use it when one behavior has multiple variants and new variants are expected over time.

column1: When should I NOT use Strategy Pattern?
column2: Do not use it when there is only one stable behavior and no expected variation.

column1: What is the core idea of Strategy Pattern?
column2: Encapsulate each algorithm in its own class behind a common interface.

column1: What are the main participants in Strategy Pattern?
column2: Context, Strategy interface, and Concrete Strategy implementations.

column1: What does the Context do in Strategy Pattern?
column2: It delegates work to the currently selected strategy.

column1: How is Strategy different from a big if-else/switch block?
column2: Strategy removes branching from one large method and localizes each behavior in its own class.

column1: What principle does Strategy support strongly?
column2: Open/Closed Principle: add new strategy classes instead of modifying existing context logic.

column1: How does Strategy reduce blast radius?
column2: Changing one algorithm affects only one strategy class instead of a central branching method.

column1: How does Strategy improve testing?
column2: Each strategy can be unit-tested in isolation; context tests focus on wiring/delegation.

column1: What is a common trade-off of Strategy Pattern?
column2: More classes/indirection, which can feel heavy for simple cases.

column1: How do you choose a strategy at runtime?
column2: Inject it (constructor/setter/factory) based on config, request type, or user input.

column1: Payment example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: PaymentContext uses PaymentStrategy interface; CardStrategy/PayPalStrategy/WalletStrategy are interchangeable.

column1: CSV example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: CsvExporter depends on Sanitizer interface; StrictSanitizer and PermissiveSanitizer are swappable strategies.

column1: How is Strategy different from Template Method?
column2: Template Method uses inheritance and fixed skeleton; Strategy uses composition and runtime-swappable behavior.

column1: How is Strategy different from State Pattern?
column2: Strategy chooses algorithm externally; State changes behavior based on internal object state transitions.

column1: What is a good naming convention for strategy interfaces?
column2: Use behavior-focused names like PaymentStrategy, PricingStrategy, Sanitizer.

column1: What is a red flag that suggests Strategy refactor?
column2: A method keeps growing with conditionals for variants and breaks often when adding a new case.

column1: Interview one-liner for Strategy Pattern?
column2: Strategy Pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, and makes them interchangeable via composition.

column1: How to explain Strategy in 30 seconds?
column2: When behavior has multiple evolving variants, put each variant behind a common interface and let a context delegate to the chosen implementation.

column1: What is behavior in OOD?
column2: Behavior is what an object does: the actions and decisions it can perform through its methods.

column1: How is behavior different from state in OOD?
column2: State is what an object knows (data/fields); behavior is what an object does (methods/logic).

column1: Why is behavior important in OOD?
column2: Good object design centers on behavior so responsibilities are clear and changes stay localized.

column1: What does "encapsulate behavior" mean?
column2: Put related logic behind a clear interface so callers use it without depending on internal details.

column1: How do you spot misplaced behavior?
column2: If a class mostly reads/writes another class's data to do work, behavior likely belongs in the other class.

column1: What is an example of behavior in a payment domain?
column2: Methods like authorize(), capture(), and refund() are behaviors because they execute business actions.

column1: What is "behavioral cohesion"?
column2: Methods in a class should serve one responsibility and change for similar reasons.

column1: How does poor behavior design increase blast radius?
column2: When unrelated behaviors are mixed in one class, small changes can break many paths and tests.

column1: In OOD, what does "tell, don't ask" mean?
column2: Tell objects to do work instead of pulling their data out and making decisions elsewhere.

column1: How is Strategy related to behavior in OOD?
column2: Strategy extracts variable behavior into interchangeable objects behind one interface.

column1: Show a minimal Strategy Pattern code example in TypeScript.
column2: interface PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string; } class CardPayment implements PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string { return `Paid $${amount} by card`; } } class PaypalPayment implements PaymentStrategy { pay(amount: number): string { return `Paid $${amount} by PayPal`; } } class PaymentContext { constructor(private strategy: PaymentStrategy) {} setStrategy(strategy: PaymentStrategy): void { this.strategy = strategy; } checkout(amount: number): string { return this.strategy.pay(amount); } } const ctx = new PaymentContext(new CardPayment()); ctx.checkout(50); ctx.setStrategy(new PaypalPayment()); ctx.checkout(50);

column1: What is a concrete real-world example of Strategy Pattern?
column2: Payment processing: `PaymentContext` depends on `PaymentStrategy`, while `CardPayment`, `PaypalPayment`, and `WalletPayment` provide different payment algorithms that can be swapped at runtime.

column1: How does the caller switch strategies in code?
column2: The caller injects a different implementation into context, e.g. `context.setStrategy(new PaypalPayment())`, then calls the same `checkout()` method.

column1: What unit tests would you write for a Strategy Pattern payment example?
column2: Test each strategy class behavior independently (Card/PayPal/Wallet), then test `PaymentContext` delegation and strategy switching without retesting payment internals.

column1: Show a concrete CSV sanitization Strategy example.
column2: interface Sanitizer { sanitize(value: string): string; } class StrictSanitizer implements Sanitizer { sanitize(v: string): string { return v.replace(/[^\w\s-]/g, ''); } } class PermissiveSanitizer implements Sanitizer { sanitize(v: string): string { return v.trim(); } } class CsvExporter { constructor(private sanitizer: Sanitizer) {} setSanitizer(s: Sanitizer): void { this.sanitizer = s; } exportCell(raw: string): string { return this.sanitizer.sanitize(raw); } }

column1: What is behavior localization in OOD?
column2: Behavior localization means keeping a behavior in one focused place, close to the data and rules it belongs to, instead of spreading it across many classes.

column1: Why is behavior localization important?
column2: It reduces blast radius, improves readability, and makes testing/debugging easier because behavior changes happen in one place.

column1: Give a delocalized behavior example.
column2: Bad example: discount logic is partly in controller, partly in service, and partly in utility methods, so changing one discount rule requires edits in multiple files.

column1: Give a localized behavior example.
column2: Good example: all discount rules live in `DiscountPolicy` (or `PricingStrategy`) and callers delegate to it, so a rule change happens in one class.

column1: How can I detect poor behavior localization?
column2: If one behavior change requires touching 3+ places, behavior is likely delocalized.

column1: How is behavior localization related to cohesion?
column2: Localized behavior increases cohesion because a class has one clear responsibility and related methods change for the same reason.

column1: How is behavior localization related to "Tell, Don't Ask"?
column2: With localization, callers tell an object to do work instead of pulling data out and doing the logic elsewhere.

column1: What is a concrete payment localization example?
column2: `PaymentContext` delegates payment processing to `PaymentStrategy`; payment algorithms are localized in `CardPayment`, `PaypalPayment`, etc., not mixed in one giant method.

column1: What is a concrete CSV localization example?
column2: `CsvExporter` delegates cell cleaning to `Sanitizer`; sanitization rules are localized in sanitizer classes instead of scattered across exporter, controller, and utility files.

column1: What is a common trade-off of behavior localization?
column2: You may introduce more classes/interfaces, but gain maintainability and safer future changes.

column1: What is indirection in OOD?
column2: Indirection means accessing behavior through an intermediate abstraction (like an interface or adapter) instead of calling a concrete implementation directly.

column1: Give a simple direct vs indirect example.
column2: Direct: `exporter.sanitize(value)` calls concrete code in the exporter. Indirect: `exporter` calls `sanitizer.sanitize(value)` through a `Sanitizer` interface.

column1: What is a good indirection example in payments?
column2: `CheckoutService` depends on `PaymentGateway` interface; concrete implementations like `StripeGateway` or `PaypalGateway` can be swapped without changing checkout logic.

column1: What is a good indirection example in notifications?
column2: `Notifier` depends on `NotificationChannel` interface; `EmailChannel`, `SmsChannel`, and `PushChannel` are interchangeable implementations.

column1: What is a good indirection example in storage?
column2: `UserRepository` depends on `UserStore` abstraction; you can switch from `PostgresUserStore` to `DynamoUserStore` without changing domain logic.

column1: How does indirection reduce coupling?
column2: Callers depend on abstractions, so implementation changes stay behind the interface and do not force caller edits.

column1: How does indirection help testing?
column2: You can inject a fake/mock implementation through the same interface to isolate and test caller behavior.

column1: What is over-indirection?
column2: Adding abstractions with no real variation or change pressure, which makes code harder to follow without clear benefit.

column1: Give an over-indirection example.
column2: Creating `ISanitizeService`, `SanitizeManager`, and `SanitizeProvider` when there is one stable sanitization rule and no expected variants.

column1: When is indirection worth the cost?
column2: When behavior is likely to vary, external dependencies may change, or you need clear seams for testing and modularity.

column1: What is the trade-off of indirection?
column2: You gain flexibility and safer change boundaries, but pay with extra classes, wiring, and mental hops during debugging.

column1: Interview one-liner: indirection in OOD?
column2: Indirection introduces an abstraction layer between caller and implementation to reduce coupling and improve substitutability, at the cost of added complexity.

column1: What is EXPLAIN in PostgreSQL?
column2: It shows the planner's estimated execution plan without running the query.

column1: What is EXPLAIN ANALYZE in PostgreSQL?
column2: It executes the query and shows actual runtime metrics for each plan node.

column1: Why use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)?
column2: It combines real timing with page-level cache/read evidence so you can diagnose where time is spent.

column1: What does Seq Scan mean?
column2: PostgreSQL is scanning table pages in order and checking rows against the filter.

column1: When can Seq Scan be the right choice?
column2: When a query matches a large fraction of rows or the table is small enough that sequential read is cheaper.

column1: What is a heap table in PostgreSQL?
column2: The heap is the main table storage where full row data is stored.

column1: What is the difference between index pages and heap pages?
column2: Index pages store key/pointer structures; heap pages store full table rows.

column1: In PostgreSQL, are pages and blocks the same concept?
column2: Yes; "page" and "block" are used interchangeably for fixed-size storage units.

column1: What is PostgreSQL block_size usually?
column2: Usually 8192 bytes (8 KB).

column1: How can you verify PostgreSQL page/block size?
column2: Run SHOW block_size;.

column1: Can PostgreSQL read one row directly from disk without touching a page?
column2: No; disk I/O is page-oriented, then rows are processed from that page in memory.

column1: How many rows fit in one page?
column2: It varies by row width, tuple overhead, dead tuples, and TOAST behavior.

column1: What does shared_blks_hit indicate?
column2: Pages were found in shared buffers (cache hits) so no storage read was needed for those pages.

column1: What does shared_blks_read indicate?
column2: Pages had to be loaded into shared buffers from the storage path (cache misses).

column1: What does blks_hit in pg_stat_database represent?
column2: Database-level count of block hits found in cache.

column1: What does blks_read in pg_stat_database represent?
column2: Database-level count of blocks read from storage path.

column1: What is cache_hit_pct in monitoring queries?
column2: A derived ratio showing how often requested blocks were served from cache.

column1: Why are pg_stat_* counters often misleading as one-time snapshots?
column2: They are cumulative totals, so you should compare deltas over a time window.

column1: What does Rows Removed by Filter tell you?
column2: How many scanned rows were discarded after predicate evaluation.

column1: What is a composite index?
column2: An index on multiple columns, such as (country, status), optimized for matching query patterns.

column1: What is the left-prefix rule for composite indexes?
column2: An index on (a,b,c) is efficiently usable for predicates starting with a, then a+b, then a+b+c.

column1: What is a covering index in PostgreSQL?
column2: An index that contains all columns needed by a query, often via INCLUDE columns.

column1: Why can a covering index speed up reads significantly?
column2: It can enable Index Only Scan and avoid heap page lookups.

column1: What does Heap Fetches: 0 mean in Index Only Scan?
column2: The query result was served from index pages without fetching rows from heap pages.

column1: What is Bitmap Heap Scan?
column2: Planner uses index results to build a bitmap of candidate heap pages, then reads those pages.

column1: Why might Bitmap Heap Scan still be slower than Index Only Scan?
column2: It still touches heap pages, while index-only can avoid those lookups.

column1: What is cache thrashing in PostgreSQL?
column2: Repeated eviction and reloading of useful pages due to competing workloads or insufficient memory.

column1: What is a practical symptom of cache thrashing?
column2: The same query alternates between fast and slow with rising shared_blks_read and disk read pressure.

column1: How can you intentionally reproduce cache thrashing in a lab?
column2: Constrain DB container memory and run competing workloads that exceed the working set.

column1: How do you find high-I/O queries quickly in production?
column2: Use pg_stat_statements and rank by shared_blks_read and total_exec_time.

column1: What is the most reliable way to confirm a DB tuning fix?
column2: Compare before/after plans and metrics using the same query shape and workload window.

column1: What trade-off comes with adding more indexes?
column2: Faster reads but slower writes, more storage use, and more maintenance work.

column1: When is a partial index useful?
column2: When queries repeatedly target a subset of rows matching a stable predicate.

column1: When is an expression index useful?
column2: When predicates apply a function (for example lower(email)) that a normal index cannot satisfy efficiently.

column1: What is one strong backend developer habit for DB performance work?
column2: Treat every optimization as a measured experiment with hypothesis, evidence, and rollback plan.

column1: What is shared_buffers in PostgreSQL?
column2: It is PostgreSQL's primary in-memory cache for table and index pages.

column1: How does shared_buffers relate to shared_blks_hit?
column2: shared_blks_hit counts page requests served from shared_buffers without needing a storage read for those pages.

column1: How does shared_buffers relate to shared_blks_read?
column2: shared_blks_read increases when needed pages are not already in shared_buffers and must be loaded through the storage path.

column1: Is shared_buffers the only cache affecting PostgreSQL reads?
column2: No; the OS page cache also affects read behavior, so effective performance reflects both caches plus workload.

column1: Why does shared_buffers matter for query latency?
column2: Higher useful cache residency reduces repeated page loads and usually lowers latency for repeated access patterns.

column1: What does Buffers: shared hit=6199 (without shared read) imply in EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
column2: The query touched many pages but served them from shared buffer cache; no storage-page reads were needed in that run.

column1: Why can a Nested Loop be a good plan for ORDER BY ... LIMIT queries?
column2: With top-N queries, planner can use an ordered outer path and stop early after enough matches, making nested probes efficient.

column1: In a join plan, does FROM orders JOIN users mean PostgreSQL fully reads orders first?
column2: No; planner chooses access paths and join order independently of SQL writing order when semantics allow.

column1: Why did PostgreSQL scan ~1600 orders to return 200 rows in the country-filtered join?
column2: It needed to probe users and discard non-matching country rows until 200 joined rows satisfied the filter.

column1: What does Memoize do in PostgreSQL join plans?
column2: It caches results of repeated parameterized lookups (for example repeated user_id probes) to avoid redoing identical index scans.

column1: How do you interpret Memoize Hits low and Misses high?
column2: The join key had low reuse in the scanned slice, so memoization provided limited benefit.

column1: What is selectivity in query planning?
column2: Selectivity is matched_rows / total_rows; lower ratios are more selective and usually more index-friendly.

column1: Why can an index become slower as selectivity decreases?
column2: As more rows match, index probes plus heap fetches and random access can cost more than one sequential table pass.

column1: How do you find the index-vs-seq-scan crossover point?
column2: Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) at multiple predicate widths (for example 1%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 40%, 60%) and compare plan type and time.

column1: Why might users(country, id) be useful while users(id, country) is often redundant?
column2: (country, id) supports country-first filtering + join, while (id, country) overlaps users_pkey(id) and does not efficiently serve country-only filtering.

column1: Why might users(country, id) still not be chosen in a join query?
column2: Query shape (especially top-N order-driven paths), data distribution, and cost estimates may still favor orders-first + users_pkey probes.

column1: What does planning time vs execution time mean in EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
column2: Planning time is optimizer work to choose a plan; execution time is running that plan and returning rows.

column1: What is a senior takeaway when a query is already ~5 ms and stable?
column2: Document why the plan is fast and avoid speculative indexes unless real workload evidence demands change.

column1: Does creating an index guarantee PostgreSQL will use it?
column2: No; planner chooses the lowest estimated total cost plan based on query shape, statistics, and available paths.

column1: What practical signals suggest an index path is degrading?
column2: Growing matched-row fraction, rising heap/page touches, increasing random reads, and eventual planner shift to bitmap/seq scan.

column1: Can PostgreSQL avoid per-query planning time in production?
column2: Yes, with prepared statements/plan caching (often via drivers): planning overhead can drop, but generic reused plans may execute worse than custom parameter-specific plans for skewed data.

column1: If a filter becomes low-selectivity (for example most rows are active), what should you do at query/app design level?
column2: Add a naturally selective predicate from request/session context (for example tenant_id/user_id + time range), then align a composite index to that access pattern (for example (tenant_id, created_at DESC)) so reads narrow early.

column1: Why did ORDER BY amount_cents DESC LIMIT 5000 use Parallel Seq Scan + Sort before indexing?
column2: Without a matching order index, PostgreSQL had to scan many rows and perform top-N sorting to discover the highest amount_cents values.

column1: Why does an index on (amount_cents DESC) speed up ORDER BY amount_cents DESC LIMIT N?
column2: The index already stores rows in required order, so PostgreSQL can walk the index and stop after N rows without full scan/sort.

column1: What plan shape change indicates a successful top-N index optimization?
column2: Before: Parallel Seq Scan + Sort + Gather Merge; After: direct Index Scan in ORDER BY direction plus early LIMIT stop.

column1: How do buffers confirm the Q4 top-N index improvement?
column2: Before showed high reads (for example shared read=2060); after index reads dropped sharply (for example shared read=13), indicating far fewer uncached pages touched.

column1: What was the approximate Q4 runtime improvement after creating idx_orders_amount_cents?
column2: Execution dropped from about 44.7 ms to about 5.25 ms, roughly an 8.5x improvement in that run.

column1: Show a production-style PostgreSQL CREATE INDEX ... INCLUDE example for quick syntax revision.
column2: Example: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_tenant_created_at_inc ON orders (tenant_id, created_at DESC) INCLUDE (status, total_cents); Use when queries filter by tenant/time and return status/total_cents so index-only scans are possible.
